methods.description: This method describes the process of using histochemical stains to assess the viability of pollen grains. The analysis is crucial for understanding pollen viability, which is important for genetic improvement programs and conservation strategies.

steps.description: Anthers collected from three flowers of three different plants were placed on a glass slide and the pollen grains were spread on the slide.
steps.searchable_summary: collect and prepare pollen samples on slides

steps.description: One drop of a given staining solution was added per slide, and the samples were covered with a coverslip for observation under a light microscope.
steps.searchable_summary: apply staining solution and prepare for microscopy

steps.description: One hundred pollen grains were counted per slide, with three replications, each consisting of one slide.
The percent of viable pollen was calculated as the average of three replicates.
The viability data were also transformed by arc sin (√x/100) prior to statistical analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: transform viability data for statistical analysis

steps.description: For comparison of means, the data was submitted to analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by the Scott-Knott test (p <0.01), using the SAS program (SAS Institute 2004).
steps.searchable_summary: perform ANOVA and Scott-Knott test on viability data
